title: Chapter 6 – Recognition (2)
content:
The lingering cold froze the dawn air on the mountain behind Jeong Estate.

Jeong Yeon-shin thought:

If the wind sweeping through the valley had a color, it would be grayish-white.

The people of Jeong Estate, who once held dominion over Hyeon, now lay beneath soil that would nourish next year's seedlings.

All through the night, they had carried and buried corpses.

Jeong Yeon-shin, the steward, a handful of surviving workers, and the Zhongnan faction experts led by Jeong Jung-san were all caked in dirt.

His niece, curled into a ball off to one side, drifted between weeping and fitful sleep.

Glancing at Hye-ah, Jeong Yeon-shin spoke.

"It's over."

"Indeed. This will be a sorrowful mountain. It didn't even have a name."

The steward agreed. Jeong Yeon-shin shook his head.

"No. I mean Jeong Estate is over."

"……"

"Master Precipice Sword, Master Shadowless Fist. Thank you again for your help."

Leaving the stricken steward behind, he bowed to the Zhongnan faction warriors.

Even coated in dirt, their eyes burned with spirit.

They returned the gesture with sincerity, offering genuine respect even to a mere boy.

"He is now your disciple's household, so it hardly feels like an imposition."

"Besides… it's hard to say we bear no responsibility for failing to prevent this."

Following Precipice Sword, Shadowless Fist spoke gravely, the weight of it settling between his thick brows.

Jeong Jung-san, preparing to depart with them, held his niece Jeong Hye and regarded his third younger brother with a somber expression.

The bloodshed seemed to have changed him, if only slightly.

"How can years of accumulated wrongs be undone with a single apology?"

Jeong Jung-san said. He looked pained.

"When we meet again, I'll try to be a proper older brother. I'll make the effort — so you survive."

"Though I'm belatedly entering Zhongnan martial arts, I'm not exceptionally talented. That's the honest truth."

For the first time in his life, Jeong Yeon-shin spoke plainly to an elder.

It felt liberating, and he wondered why he hadn't done it sooner.

"You'll have to train as if your life depends on it. And take good care of Hye-ah."

"…I understand."

And so the Zhongnan faction experts, his second older brother, and young niece departed.

Turning around, he found the steward standing with the workers, bundles in hand.

"Where will you go, steward?"

"I mentioned I had contacts. There are small branches of Jeong Estate scattered throughout Namyang. Since Jeong bloodlines still survive, it's only natural that I serve them with whatever family assets remain."

"But Jeong Estate has fallen, hasn't it?"

The steward smiled faintly.

"It's a large landowner family. The family-owned farmlands aren't confined to Xinye."

He hadn't realized the man was this loyal. He seemed too good for Jeong Estate.

"As you know, I'm heading to Desolate Sect. I hope we can see each other from time to time."

"Of course."

The steward had also studied martial arts. He should be able to look after himself.

"I hope even you live a long life, steward. Take care of yourself."

"I'll pray for your success, young master."

It was a farewell to both the steward and Jeong Estate. The steward silently watched the boy's retreating back as he walked away without once looking behind him.

'Live a long life…? Have I ever heard such words from anyone of Jeong blood after receiving the grace of past generations?'

Jeong Yeon-shin sensed the gaze but deliberately kept his head forward.

A new air surrounded his steps as he pressed through the tall grass.

* * *

It was his first time beyond Xinye County.

He could follow the official road after receiving detailed directions from the steward, but he inevitably drew a great deal of attention.

The main road, where packed-dirt paths first caught the light of dawn.

The official road connecting Namyang and Xiangyang also linked Henan and Huguang provinces.

With a major route to Shaanxi not far off, he found himself checking again and again whether the pack on his back was still intact.

That's how many people he encountered.

"His clothes are unusual for a beggar."

"Isn't that silk? Using silk for martial robes…"

"Silk? But that appearance……"

The porters from a passing convoy whispered among themselves.

He had heard that merchant groups or convoys met on the road sometimes turned into bandits.

Jeong Yeon-shin walked in silence, keeping his hand near his sword hilt.

'This is troublesome.'

But he couldn't take the less-traveled mountain paths either.

As a novice who had just stepped into the martial world, he lacked experience. Getting lost would be disastrous.

Caution alone, however, was not enough.

Wolves eyeing clothes and coin lurked everywhere — in a world where robbery and murder were commonplace.

Eventually, he clashed with wandering swordsmen, and those rogues paid for underestimating the young swordsman with their necks.

They were incomparably weak beside the warriors of the Thirteen Heavens — bewilderingly so.

'What were they counting on when they attacked?'

Though called rogues, among the martial artists commonly seen along the roadside, even those who had properly cultivated internal energy were rare.

It was a world where learning was precious.

From Xinye to Xiangyang.

A short distance for experts who could soar through the sky with lightness skills.

But for Jeong Yeon-shin, whose lightness skills were limited, the journey was a crucible of the martial world's hardships and bloody, real combat.

Among them, no one could block a single stroke of his swift sword, and as he cut down rogues one after another, the Jeong family's pupil technique melded ever deeper into his swordsmanship.

It was different from training the body alone.

As he wielded his sword in real combat while employing the pupil technique, his body transformed once more.

Muscles throughout his frame adapted to instantaneous explosive power, and his flexibility sharpened even further.

Swift sword, swift sword, and more swift sword.

The swift sword technique, already fast, now moved beyond even his own eyes' ability to follow.

As his body — the very foundation of martial arts — actively evolved, the effectiveness of his vital energy was inevitably amplified.

'They called it divine skill.'

Perhaps owing to the merit of having practiced the Jeong family's pupil technique for a short lifetime, he could visually confirm the changes occurring in his transforming body.

One week.

A stretch of enduring his first nights sleeping rough, hard beds, and the chill of open air.

"Phew."

The boy sighed as he entered an inn and set down his belongings. He had arrived in Xiangyang.

A city that had thrived and prospered since the establishment of Desolate Sect.

Tens of thousands of people passed through daily, and thousands of types of goods changed hands.

By trade volume and cultural sophistication, it ranked among the foremost metropolises of the Central Plains.

"Business is even better now! That Form fellow is really having a field day! They say they can't brew enough to keep up!"

It was a conversation between what seemed to be merchants.

Jeong Yeon-shin, who had secured a room and come downstairs to order a meal, listened closely.

"People flock from all over the Central Plains chasing dreams of success. I've even heard of martial artists throwing silver around like water."

"These days, taverns are packed day and night. Martial artists who came for the entrance exam are drowning themselves in drink!"

"Desolate Sect really is something."

"Of course! It's like they snatched dying Xiangyang from the brink and fed it until it was bursting!"

"What a way to put it! Hahaha!"

Jeong Yeon-shin agreed with at least the last observation.

He could feel the sky-high cost of living from the lodging fee alone, and it was obvious his money would run dry within another month at this rate.

He had thought he'd brought a generous sum, quietly set aside from the Blade Sect.

That's when it happened.

"Did you also come to enter Desolate Sect, young brother?"

A young man sat down across from him with an easy smile.

The sword at his waist was clearly no ordinary blade, even at a glance, and above all, the blue band around his head was striking.

An item called the hero's headband.

These days, it was an accessory no one wore unless they were truly trying to make a statement, and this one even bore a dragon embroidered in gold thread.

"And you are?"

"Ah! I didn't introduce myself. I was just curious when I spotted a challenger younger than me."

The young man continued, his face open and smiling.

"I'm Heon Won-chang. I came from far-off Shanxi."

"I'm Jeong Yeon-shin from Xinye."

"Xinye? Where is that?"

"A county in Namyang, Hebei."

"So you came from nearby! The journey must have been easy. I crossed swords with bandits in the green forests and raced river pirates through the rapids! Their water skills were fierce!"

"Well… I suppose mine was comfortable."

Jeong Yeon-shin let it pass without elaboration.

"Right — so it seems you're a comrade preparing for the Desolate Sect entrance."

"What's your business?"

"Just looking to make acquaintances! Trade whatever information we might have. Almost no one passes the Desolate Sect test on their first try. It might happen once in a thousand attempts, and even then, it's usually prodigies from renowned martial families. I know that most people get through only after accumulating two, three, even ten attempts."

"There's a test?"

At those words, Heon Won-chang's eyes went wide, as if to say, 'You didn't know?' The exaggerated expression suited him perfectly.

"It's called the Desolate test! Desolate Sect itself is unlike ordinary sects. Just as you need to take the military or civil service exam to enter official positions, there's a separate test to enter Desolate Sect."

"So that's why there are so many people…"

"Whether you have a foundation or not, the moment you're admitted, a world of learning and opportunity opens up. It's no wonder martial artists from across the land flock here. There must be thousands at any given time, practicing all manner of martial arts to pass."

Desolate Sect.

If you want to challenge the world with a single weapon, go to Desolate Sect — even officials will think twice before crossing you.

It was a common saying. A cradle of martial arts that would set any heart racing.

As the imperial court that ruled the Central Plains moved directly to suppress unruly martial sects, the Sect was said to overflow with precious medicines and advanced martial arts gathered from all nine provinces.

"Some people still place more value on the traditional large sects. Truly ignorant talk. A sect founded by mere commoners is stronger than Desolate Sect? If that were so, those factions would establish empires and play emperor themselves — how, then, would the Great Ming Empire still stand? Those groups who collect protection money and donations in their own territories and lord over their regions like petty kings."

"I see."

Jeong Yeon-shin simply went along with it. He wondered if this person held a grudge against the large sects.

"Anyway, coming to take the Iphwang test is an excellent choice. …If you can pass, that is."

His expression turned suddenly gloomy.

Jeong Yeon-shin found the Heon Won-chang sitting before him quite interesting.

It was the first time he'd encountered such a chatterbox whose moods swung so wildly.

"If you pass, you'll immediately earn a reputation as a rising talent — the best among juniors your age! A martial artist of your years in Desolate Sect would undeniably be an outstanding young master!"

The energy drained from Heon Won-chang's voice once more as the smile returned to his face.

"…But the walls of Desolate Sect are dizzyingly high, as much as its prestige."

"Yes. I see."

Just as Jeong Yeon-shin was beginning to grow bored, the food arrived.

Kung pao chicken and vegetable dishes with small greens.

Having not eaten properly in days, even the aroma was a relief.

Heon Won-chang, who had been watching him eat with a complicated expression, spoke up.

"So, how about we go take the test together tomorrow?"

"Sure, let's do that."

Jeong Yeon-shin answered without much thought.

In truth, he was glad to learn that entry required nothing more than passing a test.

He had no desire to go begging help from maternal relatives whose faces and whereabouts he didn't even know.

It was both a resolution and youthful pride — he would not become pitiful, even if his life hung in the balance.

* * *

The next day.

"We've come to the right place."

Standing before Desolate Sect, Jeong Yeon-shin was no longer the third son of Jeong Estate.

A swordsman who had weathered an especially brutal week for a boy of means. The boy had become a martial artist.

'It's enormous.'

Looking up at the main gate, he marveled. The scale surpassed anything he'd ever heard of.

The fortress spread its massive presence wide, its shoulders pressed against the azure of a clear sky.

"Impregnable."

Heon Won-chang murmured beside him, mesmerized. Jeong Yeon-shin silently agreed.

The double-layered, towering walls looked immovable.

A form that enclosed an area so vast its beginning and end lay far apart.

Around it ran a moat, wrapping the fortress in a broad band of water.

The wide-open, massive main gate was the confidence of a place that claimed to be the finest in the martial world.

Jeong Yeon-shin stepped forward.

There was a fenced training ground out front. This must be the site of the first test Heon Won-chang had mentioned.

"Let's go."

"Indeed."

The two approached the reception desk at the very front and gave their particulars.

The receptionist, dressed in scholar's attire, noted it down and confirmed.

"Jeong Yeon-shin from Xinye County, Namyang, Henan Province, correct?"

"Yes."

"Enter the training ground."

He received one of the round tokens stacked on one side of the sizable desk and turned away.

The token bore the character thirteen (十三).

'It's loud.'

It wasn't only examiners and applicants gathered here.

Beyond the young martial artists dreaming of ascent through this gateway, there were throngs of spectators, merchants, and gamblers.

Desolate Sect made no effort to turn them away, as if inviting the public to watch and enjoy.

"Over here."

As he passed through the fence gate alongside Heon Won-chang, a martial artist overseeing the test called to them.

Neat martial clothes and a firmly condensed aura. The man's presence was no less imposing than the experts of the Thirteen Heavens.

The examiner said:

"The first test is the swift sword form."

"Pardon?"

…
